risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group" and "the randomization process has been described in detail [23]. The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: Although this paper does not spell out the exact random sequence method in the excerpt provided, it explicitly states that participants were randomized and refers to a detailed prior description of the randomization process. In Cochrane risk-of-bias assessment, this is sufficient context to support a proper random allocation process rather than a non-random or predictable method.

Summary: The paper states that participants were randomized and that "The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded," but it gives no explicit description of how the allocation sequence was concealed from the recruiter/enroller. The report does not mention central allocation, sealed opaque sequential envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge. Comment: Because the study describes randomization and blinding of the allocator but not allocation concealment, the risk of selection bias from foreknowledge of assignment remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group." and "There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group." Comment: Participants clearly knew which group they were in (some control participants preferred the exercise group), and the intervention was an obvious exercise programme, so blinding of participants/personnel was not feasible. Knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence subjective outcomes such as pain, well-being, and questionnaire-based quality of life, and could also affect effort during performance tests.

Quote: "The same physiotherapist, who was blinded to the previous measurement, performed all measurements." Comment: Outcome assessment for the objective/observer-measured outcomes (range of motion, balance, muscle strength, step-test) was carried out by a physiotherapist who was blinded to prior measurements; these outcomes were measured with standardized instruments and are unlikely to have been influenced by knowledge of allocation. The patient-reported questionnaires (CHAQ/CHQ) were self-reported by participants, but the major assessed outcomes in this domain were measured by the blinded physiotherapist, so detection bias is low.

Quote: "There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group. There were another six dropouts after the first test occasion due to the families’ lack of time." Comment: Attrition was explicitly present and was not balanced at randomization-stage dropout: all 10 initial dropouts were from the control group, and an additional 6 participants dropped out later for logistical reasons. This creates clear post-randomization loss with imbalance between groups, especially at the start of follow-up, and could bias outcome estimates.
